prompt: My team wants to use a neural network for a sales prediction problem. We haven't tried linear regression yet. Is that the right order?
expected: Skill activates and says no: start with the simplest plausible model (linear regression) to establish a measured baseline metric first, then only step up to a neural network if that baseline fails the pre-defined threshold — complexity must be justified by measured test-set improvement.

prompt: I added a new feature to my regression model but the test MAE didn't improve. Should I keep the feature?
expected: Skill activates and says remove the feature: an unchanged test MAE means the added complexity has zero measured benefit while incurring opacity and maintenance cost — this is exactly the Newspaper example, where the feature is rejected by measurement rather than intuition.

prompt: What's the right process for deciding when to increase ML model complexity?
expected: Skill activates and gives the ratchet process: define the metric and threshold first, implement the simplest plausible model, measure its test-set metric and stop if it meets the threshold, otherwise step up complexity one level and re-measure — documenting each step (model, features, metric, keep/reject) and rejecting any step that does not improve the metric by a meaningful margin.

prompt: How do I implement a convolutional neural network in Go?
What's the difference between L1 and L2 regularization?
The simplest model for our NLP task would be TF-IDF plus logistic regression. Should we start there even though BERT would probably do better?
expected: Skill activates and says yes, start with TF-IDF + logistic regression to establish the baseline; BERT is only justified if that baseline fails the metric threshold. It should note the boundary caveat that in NLP a pre-trained model can itself be the 'simple' starting point, but the discipline is the same — measure first, then decide if complexity is needed.

prompt: My simple model meets the accuracy threshold but a more complex model has 2% better accuracy. Should I use the complex model?
expected: Skill activates and defaults to the simpler model because the threshold is already met — a numerically higher score is not the gate. It should surface that the 2% must be weighed against added maintenance cost, interpretability loss, and overfitting risk, and ask whether 2% is meaningful in the business context.

prompt: We tried logistic regression, random forest, and XGBoost. All perform similarly. Which should we use in production?
expected: Skill activates and selects logistic regression, the simplest model, since the metrics are equivalent — complexity is not justified without measured improvement, and interpretability and lower maintenance cost favor the simpler model.

prompt: Training accuracy is 98% with our complex model but test accuracy is 72%. Is this what the simplest-model principle is designed to catch?
expected: Skill activates and confirms yes: the large train-test gap is overfitting — the complex model memorized the training data. A simpler model would likely have a smaller gap, and baseline-first prevents this failure mode by gating every complexity step on the honest test-set metric rather than training-set performance.
